risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "randomisation was carried out using sealed envelopes." Comment: The study explicitly reports randomisation and use of sealed envelopes, which in this context supports a proper random allocation process. Although the exact sequence-generation method is not fully described, the report provides enough context to judge the sequence generation as likely random rather than predictable or non-random.

Quote: "we designed the study to randomly compare the two groups, and randomisation was carried out using sealed envelopes." Comment: The study explicitly reports use of sealed envelopes for allocation, which is the only concealment mechanism described. Although it does not specify "opaque" or "sequentially numbered," the review characteristics record allocation concealment as "envelopes," and there is no indication that recruiters could foresee assignments. Given the provided evidence, this is the closest explicit concealment method and is treated as adequate concealment in this record.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Fraction of inspired oxygen was manually adjusted in the control group, which is the traditional routine for neonatal care in our NICU, and in the other group, it was controlled by the Auto-Mixer®." and "The bedside healthcare providers manually adjusted the FiO2 in line with our standard NICU practice." Comment: Participants and bedside personnel were not blinded to allocation, because the intervention was an obvious automated device versus routine manual adjustment. The main outcome (percentage of time SpO2 was within target, hyperoxia, FiO2 variability) could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation through different care behavior and manual adjustments, so performance bias is high.

Quote: "Oxygen saturation was continuously measured in each infant using the monitor with signal extraction technology..." and "During the 12-h period of the study, we recorded the SpO2 and FiO2 values for each infant in real time, using a computer set-up for this purpose." Comment: The outcome was assessed by automated monitor/computer recording rather than by a subjective human judgement. Although the report does not explicitly state that an assessor was blinded, the primary outcome (percentage of time SpO2 was within target range, plus related oxygen saturation metrics) was obtained from automated recordings, so knowledge of allocation would be unlikely to influence measurement.

Quote: "Ten infants were randomised to each group and completed the 12-h study period." Comment: There was essentially complete follow-up for the randomized participants, with no post-randomization losses, exclusions, or differential dropout reported. The study was very short (12 hours), and the reported data indicate that all 20 randomized infants contributed outcome data, so attrition is unlikely to have biased the result.
